title: Bank Statement Analysis for Wholesale Distributors
meta: Wholesale distributors move high volume on thin margins with heavy inventory and net terms. Learn how lenders analyze distributor bank statements for inventory financing.
content:
Distribution: Volume Business, Thin Margins

Wholesale distributors buy in bulk and resell to retailers and businesses, earning a small percentage on large turnover. The bank statement shows big inflows and big outflows with a modest spread — and that spread, not the gross volume, is what services debt.

How Distributor Revenue Appears

  • Customer payments: ACH and checks from retail and B2B accounts, frequently on net terms.
  • Supplier/manufacturer outflows: Large recurring inventory purchases.
  • Freight and logistics: Significant shipping and warehousing costs.
  • Seasonal inventory builds: Cash tied up in stock ahead of peak selling.

The Margin and Receivables Reality

With gross margins often in the 10-25% range, a distributor doing $1M/month in deposits may keep a fraction of that. Layered on top is a receivables cycle — customers pay on terms while suppliers want prompt payment. Underwriters must read the spread and the timing, not the headline volume.

What Lenders Focus On

  • True spread between customer inflows and supplier outflows
  • Customer concentration and payment reliability
  • Inventory-build timing vs. selling season
  • NSF and line-of-credit usage patterns

Bottom Line

Distributors are fundable for inventory financing and lines of credit, but only when the thin margin and receivables timing are understood. StatementScrub separates inventory pass-through from true margin automatically.
